blurb:
Cody lasted two months in his first adoption before his family had to be honest with themselves: this dog needs more than they could give him. To their credit, they brought him back rather than letting him bounce around miserable. Now he is looking for round two, and this time the right fit.

He is a three-year-old cattle dog mix, about 33 pounds, and he has got the energy you would expect from that breed. Volunteers describe him as "a strong and lovable guy with a sweet nature," and that tracks. He loves other dogs, went to doggie daycare and had a blast, and he has the kind of playful, mouthy style that is pretty standard for cattle dog mixes. He calmed right down for his shelter bath and soaked up every bit of attention that came with it. The energy is real but it is not chaos.

He went through the Mostly Mutts Prison Pups training program, so he comes with actual skills. No toddlers, he is strong and will knock a small kid over without meaning to. Older kids should be fine, and he liked the ones he met. Cats are unknown.

If you run, hike, play fetch, or just want a dog who is genuinely going to keep up with you, Cody is worth meeting. He went through Mostly Mutts once already and landed with people who loved him but could not keep up. The right household is out there. Visit mostlymutts.org to start the process.
scoring_notes:
• Prison Pups trained cattle dog with proven daycare social skills — strong fit for active households and a clear, targetable audience
• The honest-return framing works well but the story is more situational than emotional, making it solid but not the most compelling in the group
• Returned after two months with energy needs unmet, back in shelter without a foster placement, suggesting moderate wait time and real need for the right active home to see this feature

blurb:
She came back to the shelter scared and shut down, a completely different dog than the one they had adopted out nine months earlier. Something happened in that home that knocked her confidence sideways. But here is the thing about Cecelia: she is already turning it around.

She is three years old, about 37 pounds, a terrier and boxer mix currently living in a foster home and doing great. Once she trusts you, she does this full-body wag, the kind where her whole back end gets involved. She thinks she is a lap dog. She is not entirely wrong about that. Her foster family says she is the perfect housemate. The "high energy" label from her previous home? Not what they are seeing at all.

She does need some patience up front, especially with new people and especially with men early on. She is not going to rush to you on day one, and that is fine. Do not reach over her head or grab her collar before she is ready. Let her come to you and she will. She would do best as your only dog at home, and Mostly Mutts recommends calmer kids fifteen and up given where she is in her confidence-building right now.

If you have the patience to let a dog come to you on her own terms, Cecelia is going to pay that back in full. She is in foster and in training through Mostly Mutts University. Reach out through their website to set up a meet.
scoring_notes:
• Return rescue with a trauma arc already reversing in foster care — the full-body-wag payoff is exactly the emotional hook that drives newsletter shares and donations
• Highest story score in the group: a dog returned scared, rebuilding trust, and defying her previous labels is a genuinely compelling redemption narrative
• Returned after nine months, currently in foster and active training, with significant placement restrictions suggesting she has been waiting and needs the right reader to find her

blurb:
Eight years old and she still makes the cutest little noises in her sleep, tiny foot kicks and all. Glynnis is a medium-sized terrier mix who came in through animal control and has spent her time since then melting volunteers with kisses and reminding everyone what a genuinely good dog looks like. Her foster called her "the sweetest dog ever," and she is not the first person to say something like that about this girl.

She loves scratches, gives kisses freely, and has this easy, gentle energy that makes her feel immediately comfortable to be around. She went through the Mostly Mutts Prison Pups training program, so she comes with real skills and real manners. She will alert you when someone comes to the door. She will also curl up with you after.

A couple of things worth knowing. Glynnis does best with male dogs rather than female, and she tried to chase a cat in her foster home, so a cat-free house is the better fit. Kids ten and up should be great. She has already met a couple of young boys and did well. Her energy sits in the middle of the road, happy to do something or happy to just be near you.

Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw. You can start the process on their website or head over in person. Ask for Glynnis by name.
scoring_notes:
• Prison Pups training credential plus volunteer-consensus praise makes her an unusually well-packaged senior dog with immediate curb appeal
• The sleeping-with-tiny-foot-kicks detail is the kind of specific, visual storytelling that stops newsletter readers mid-scroll
• Eight-year-old animal control intake with no adoption pending signals significant shelter time — high urgency and strong editorial case for featuring her now

blurb:
Someone found Antigua and her siblings outside without their mom and got them to Mostly Mutts. They spent time in a foster home, then moved into the shelter's Kitty Room, where Antigua has been busy doing what two-and-a-half-month-old kittens do: playing hard and then climbing into a lap the second play time ends.

She's about two pounds right now and still growing. The volunteers say she splits her time pretty evenly between chasing toys and looking for someone to snuggle with, which sounds like a pretty solid schedule honestly. She's been great with her siblings and the other kittens in the shelter, so if you've got a cat at home who might enjoy a young companion, she's worth considering.

A few practical things: she's had her first round of kitten shots but will need the rest completed as she grows, and she's microchipped. She's not yet spayed due to her size, but it's required and will be done before she goes home. Gentle kids are welcome. Dog compatibility is unknown, so a meet and greet would be the move if you've got one.

Antigua is at the Mostly Mutts shelter in the Kitty Room. Stop by or check their website to set up a visit.
shelter_address: 1000 Cobb Place Blvd NW, Suite 450, Kennesaw, GA 30144
shelter_hours: Check mostlymutts.org for current hours and adoption appointment availability
scoring_notes:
• Classic kitten profile — playful, snuggly, cat-friendly, and kid-tolerant — delivers reliable newsletter click-and-share appeal
• Orphaned-outside origin adds a soft emotional hook but is not unusual enough to stand alone as a compelling narrative
• Appears to have arrived recently with siblings still present, indicating low shelter time and limited urgency relative to other candidates

blurb:
Taffeta came into her foster home as the smallest of the crew, a little behind the others in size and confidence, so she'd hang back and watch her siblings tear around the room. Not scared. Just taking it all in on her own timeline. That independent streak stuck with her even after all her siblings got adopted and moved on.

Now she's at the shelter in the Kitty Aquarium, making new friends and coming into her own. The volunteers say her playful side is showing up more and more, but she's still got that quiet confidence thing going. Her foster called her just a great kitten who will make a great addition to a home, and honestly that's the most useful thing anyone can say about a cat.

She's about two and a half months old and only around a pound and a half, so she's got some growing to do. She does well with other cats and might genuinely enjoy having a feline companion at home. Kids are fine as long as they're gentle and know how to handle a small kitten. She's litter trained and ready to go.

Taffeta is at the Mostly Mutts shelter now, so you can go meet her in person. Check their website to confirm hours or set up a visit.
shelter_address: 1000 Cobb Place Blvd NW, Suite 450, Kennesaw, GA 30144
shelter_hours: Check mostlymutts.org for current hours and adoption appointment availability
scoring_notes:
• Young, kitten-friendly, and cat-compatible — broad household appeal makes her one of the most adoptable candidates in the group
• The quiet-observer-sibling angle is charming but relatively common for kitten blurbs, limiting story distinctiveness
• Recently moved from foster to shelter with siblings already placed, suggesting she is the last one waiting and carry mild urgency

blurb:
Twiggy has been around the block once already. She was adopted from Mostly Mutts years ago, had a good home, and then a new cat moved in and things didn't click. So here she is, back at the rescue and ready to try again. She's a seven-year-old tortoiseshell, about 12 pounds, and she's been decompressing in a foster home after her world got turned upside down.

She was a little spicy when she first arrived, which is completely fair. That's what cats do when everything changes at once. But the foster home is doing its job, and the volunteers say she loves attention once she knows you. Give her time to settle in and she'll want that connection.

Here's the honest part: Twiggy would really do best as your only cat. The last situation didn't work out because of another kitty, so she's not looking for a housemate. A dog might be okay depending on the dog, but she'd need a proper introduction first. If you've got a quiet home and room for one cat who just wants to be your cat, that's exactly what she's looking for.

Twiggy is spayed, microchipped, vaccinated, and combo tested negative. Adoption fee is $175. She's currently in foster care through Mostly Mutts, so reach out through their website to set up a meeting.
shelter_address: 1000 Cobb Place Blvd NW, Suite 450, Kennesaw, GA 30144
shelter_hours: Check mostlymutts.org for current hours and adoption appointment availability
scoring_notes:
• Only-cat requirement narrows the pool but makes her a perfect match for solo-cat households — a targeted but real audience
• Return adoption story with honest context creates genuine reader empathy and trust in the rescue's transparency
• Seven-year-old return adult in foster care signals she has been waiting and needs urgent newsletter visibility to find her second chance
